title: Hilbert schemes, polygraphs, and the Macdonald positivity conjecture
overall_summary: Haiman proves that the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n, the reduced fiber product of the Hilbert scheme H_n = Hilb^n(C^2) with (C^2)^n over the symmetric power S^n C^2, is normal, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein. The proof combines Hilbert-scheme geometry, a nested-Hilbert-scheme induction, duality, and a substantial commutative-algebra theorem asserting freeness of coordinate rings of certain subspace arrangements called polygraphs. The main algebraic consequence is the n! theorem and the graded character formula identifying transformed Kostka-Macdonald coefficients with bigraded S_n-character multiplicities in the derivative span D_µ of a lattice determinant. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as a source of rigorous representation-theoretic interpretations of multigraded Hilbert series coefficients via Frobenius characteristics, Macdonald positivity, character sheaves, and diagonal coinvariant-type modules; however, it does not itself state a hook-shape combinatorial formula for the universal Schur coefficients c_λ(n) of m-set coinvariant algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1.1, characterization of transformed Macdonald polynomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition µ of n, let µ′ be the conjugate partition, let s_λ be the Schur function, and let ≥ denote dominance order on partitions of n. The transformed integral-form Macdonald polynomial H̃_µ(x;q,t), defined in the paper by H̃_µ(x;q,t) = t^{n(µ)} J_µ[X/(1−t^{−1}); q,t^{−1}], is uniquely characterized by the three conditions: (1) H̃_µ(x;q,t) lies in the Q(q,t)-span of the functions s_λ[X/(1−q)] with λ ≥ µ; (2) H̃_µ(x;q,t) lies in the Q(q,t)-span of the functions s_λ[X/(1−t)] with λ ≥ µ′; and (3) H̃_µ[1;q,t] = 1, where square brackets denote plethystic substitution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this characterization from Haiman's earlier work and uses it as the final recognition criterion in the proof of the graded character theorem. The strategy later is to construct a symmetric function F_{R_µ}(x;q,t), the Frobenius series of a doubly graded S_n-module R_µ, and verify precisely these three properties. The q = 0 or y-degree-zero quotient supplies condition (1) through the known Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber model and Hall-Littlewood triangularity; symmetry in x and y supplies condition (2); and the fact that only constants are S_n-invariant supplies condition (3).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, n! theorem, Macdonald positivity, and graded character formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a partition of n. Let D(µ) = {(i,j) in N × N : j < µ_{i+1}} be its diagram with rows and columns indexed from 0, and define the lattice determinant Δ_µ(x,y) = det(x_i^{p_j} y_i^{q_j})_{1≤i,j≤n}, where (p_j,q_j) are the cells of D(µ) in some fixed order. Let D_µ = C[∂x,∂y]Δ_µ be the vector space spanned by all partial derivatives of Δ_µ in the polynomial ring C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]. The symmetric group S_n acts by simultaneously permuting the pairs (x_i,y_i), and D_µ is doubly graded by x-degree and y-degree. If χ_λ is the irreducible S_n-character indexed by λ, then for all partitions λ,µ of n, Haiman proves the formula H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) = ∑_{r,s} t^r q^s ⟨χ_λ, ch((D_µ)_{r,s})⟩. Consequently H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) is a polynomial with nonnegative integer coefficients, and dim_C D_µ = n! for every partition µ of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is obtained after the geometric Theorem 1. Define J_µ = {p in C[x,y] : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ = 0} and R_µ = C[x,y]/J_µ. A duality argument from earlier work gives ch((R_µ)_{r,s}) = ch((D_µ)_{r,s}), so it suffices to compute the Frobenius series F_{R_µ}(x;q,t). Theorem 1 implies that X_n is Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein; Proposition 3.7.3 then identifies R_µ with the coordinate ring of the scheme-theoretic fiber of X_n over the monomial ideal I_µ in H_n. Let S_µ = O_{X_n,Q_µ} be the local ring at the unique point Q_µ over I_µ. Flatness relates the Frobenius series of S_µ and R_µ by a scalar Hilbert-series factor coming from O_{H_n,I_µ}. The global functions y_1,...,y_n form a regular sequence in S_µ, giving the plethystic relation F_{S_µ/(y)}(x;q,t) = F_{S_µ}[X(1−q);q,t]. The quotient R_µ/(y) is the Garsia-Procesi ring for the Springer fiber of type µ, whose Frobenius series is the transformed Hall-Littlewood polynomial t^{n(µ)}Q_µ[X/(1−t^{−1});t^{−1}], so only χ_λ with λ ≥ µ occur. This verifies the first triangularity condition in Proposition 2.1.1 for F_{R_µ}; the x/y symmetry verifies the second condition; and the only invariant elements of R_µ are constants, verifying normalization. Hence F_{R_µ} = H̃_µ, yielding the stated graded character formula and positivity. Setting q=t=1 recovers the regular representation dimension n!.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4.1 and Proposition 3.4.2, blowup descriptions of H_n and X_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S = C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n] and let A = S^ε be the subspace of S_n-alternating polynomials. Equivalently, A has basis the determinants Δ_D = det(x_i^{p_j}y_i^{q_j}) indexed by n-element subsets D of N × N. Let A_d be the span of products of d elements of A, with A_0 = S^{S_n}, and set T = ⊕_{d≥0} A_d as a graded S^{S_n}-algebra. Then H_n is isomorphic, as a scheme projective over S^n C^2 = Spec S^{S_n}, to Proj T. If J = S A is the ideal generated by all alternating polynomials, then the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n is isomorphic, as a scheme over (C^2)^n = Spec S, to the blowup Proj S[tJ] of (C^2)^n along J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Hilbert-scheme Proj description is quoted from Haiman's earlier work. For X_n, the Rees algebra S[tJ] is generated by S and the homogeneous subalgebra T, because A_d generates J^d as an S-module. Therefore Proj S[tJ] is a closed subscheme of the scheme-theoretic fiber product H_n ×_{S^n C^2} (C^2)^n. Since Proj S[tJ] is reduced and has the same generic locus as the reduced fiber product defining X_n, and since X_n is irreducible of dimension 2n, the closed subscheme is all of X_n. This blowup model is then used to study normality and the canonical sheaf.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.1, annihilator criterion using alternation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition µ of n, let J_µ = {p in C[x,y] : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ = 0}. Let Θ_ε be the alternation operator Θ_ε(g) = ∑_{w in S_n} ε(w) w g, where ε is the sign character. Then J_µ is exactly the set of polynomials p in C[x,y] such that for every g in C[x,y], the coefficient of Δ_µ in the alternating polynomial Θ_ε(gp), expanded in the determinant basis {Δ_D}, is zero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result is cited from Haiman's earlier paper. The underlying idea is that the determinant basis {Δ_D} identifies alternating polynomials with the exterior power ∧^n C[x,y], and the differential pairing between polynomials and constant-coefficient differential operators tests coefficients in this basis. A polynomial p annihilates Δ_µ under the differential action exactly when every product gp has zero projection, after alternation, onto the Δ_µ line. This criterion is the bridge between the local fiber of the Hilbert-scheme construction and the explicit differential annihilator ideal J_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.2, ideal sheaf of X_n inside the n-fold universal family
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be the universal family over H_n, let B = π_*O_F be the tautological rank n bundle, and identify the relative n-fold product F^n over H_n with Spec B^{⊗n}. Let X_n be considered as a closed reduced subscheme of F^n, with ideal sheaf J in B^{⊗n}. Multiplication in B^{⊗n}, followed by alternation, gives an O_{H_n}-bilinear map B^{⊗n} ⊗ B^{⊗n} → B^{⊗n} → ∧^n B. Let φ: B^{⊗n} → (B^{⊗n})^* ⊗ ∧^n B be the induced homomorphism. Then the ideal sheaf J of X_n in F^n is precisely ker φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: On the generic open subset U of H_n corresponding to n distinct reduced points, F^n is reduced and decomposes into n! points over each ideal, and X_n is the component corresponding to ordered spectra. A section s of B^{⊗n} belongs to the ideal of X_n iff its restriction vanishes on the generic part W = ρ^{−1}(U) of X_n. If s vanishes on X_n, then for every section g, the alternating section Θ_ε(gs) vanishes on X_n and also vanishes on the other components of F^n because it is alternating and hence zero when two points coincide; thus Θ_ε(gs)=0. Conversely, if s does not vanish at some generic ordered point Q, one chooses g to vanish on all other points of the S_n-orbit of Q but not at Q; then Θ_ε(gs) is nonzero. Therefore the vanishing ideal is exactly the kernel of the alternation pairing.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.3, local geometry at monomial ideals and the n! conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I_µ be the monomial ideal in C[x,y] whose standard monomials are indexed by the diagram D(µ), and let Q_µ be the unique point of X_n lying over I_µ in H_n. The following are equivalent: (1) X_n is locally Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein at Q_µ; (2) dim_C D_µ = n!, where D_µ = C[∂x,∂y]Δ_µ. When these equivalent conditions hold, the ideal of the scheme-theoretic fiber ρ^{−1}(I_µ) ⊂ X_n, viewed as a closed subscheme of (C^2)^n, is exactly J_µ = {p : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ=0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the bundle map φ from Proposition 3.7.2. At a point I in H_n, φ specializes to φ(I): B^{⊗n}(I) → B^{⊗n}(I)^* ⊗ ∧^nB(I), and the rank of φ(I) is lower semicontinuous; generically it is n!. For I=I_µ, compose the quotient map C[x,y] → (C[x,y]/I_µ)^{⊗n} with φ(I_µ) to obtain η. The alternating functional λ: A → ∧^n(C[x,y]/I_µ) kills every determinant Δ_D except Δ_µ, so Proposition 3.7.1 gives ker η = J_µ. If dim C[x,y]/J_µ = n!, then rank φ(I_µ)=n!, hence the rank is locally constant near I_µ; im φ = ρ_*O_{X_n} is locally free, so ρ is flat and X_n is Cohen-Macaulay near Q_µ. The fiber algebra is C[x,y]/J_µ, known from Emsalem to be Gorenstein, so the local ring is Gorenstein. Conversely, if X_n is locally Gorenstein at Q_µ, quotient its local ring by the parameter ideal from O_{H_n,I_µ}; the result is a Gorenstein Artin quotient C[x,y]/J of length n!. Since ρ_*O_{X_n} is locally free and generically the regular S_n-representation, every fiber affords the regular representation. The socle is one-dimensional and S_n-stable, hence must be the alternating line rather than constants. The factorization through η gives J ⊂ J_µ. If strict containment held, the socle would map into J_µ/J, forcing the alternating part of C[x,y]/J_µ to vanish and hence Δ_µ in J_µ, impossible. Thus J=J_µ and dim C[x,y]/J_µ=n!.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, main geometric theorem on the isospectral Hilbert scheme
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_n = Hilb^n(C^2) and let X_n be the reduced fiber product of H_n with (C^2)^n over S^n C^2, whose closed points are tuples (I,P_1,...,P_n) with σ(I)=⟦P_1,...,P_n⟧. Then X_n is normal, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein. Moreover its canonical sheaf is O_{X_n}(−1), where O(1) is the natural ample line bundle inherited from the blowup construction X_n = Proj S[tJ].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Normality is obtained from the blowup model X_n=Proj S[tJ]. Proposition 3.8.1 implies every power J^d of the alternant ideal is free over C[y_1,...,y_n], and Corollary 3.8.3 identifies J^d with the intersection ∩_{i<j}(x_i−x_j,y_i−y_j)^d. Since powers of complete-intersection diagonal ideals are integrally closed and intersections of integrally closed ideals are integrally closed, all J^d are integrally closed; hence the Rees algebra S[tJ] is normal. The Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein properties are proved by induction on n. The cases n≤3 are handled directly via the n! statement in small rank. Assuming the result for n−1, form the nested isospectral Hilbert scheme X_{n−1,n}=H_{n−1,n} ×_{H_{n−1}} X_{n−1}; flatness of X_{n−1} over H_{n−1} makes X_{n−1,n} Cohen-Macaulay, and the canonical-bundle computation for H_{n−1,n} gives ω_{X_{n−1,n}}=O(0,−1). Consider the proper projection g:X_{n−1,n}→X_n. Lemma 3.8.5 is applied with z_i=y_i−y_n for i=1,...,n−1. The z_i form a regular sequence on X_n because X_n is flat over Spec C[y] by Corollary 3.8.2, and on X_{n−1,n} because this scheme is Cohen-Macaulay and its y=0 locus has the expected dimension. Away from V(z_i), product decompositions reduce Rg_*O=O to the induction hypothesis. The fiber-dimension condition follows from the estimate r ≥ binom(d+2,2) for the nested Hilbert scheme. Therefore Rg_*O_{X_{n−1,n}}=O_{X_n}. Grothendieck duality then pushes the dualizing complex O(0,−1)[2n] forward to O(−1)[2n], showing that X_n is Gorenstein with canonical sheaf O(−1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8.5, proper pushforward criterion via regular sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let g:Y→X be a proper morphism of schemes. Let z_1,...,z_m be global regular functions on X, also viewed on Y by pullback. Let Z=V(z_1,...,z_m)⊂X and U=X∖Z. Suppose: (1) z_1,...,z_m is a regular sequence in O_{X,P} for every P in Z; (2) z_1,...,z_m is a regular sequence in O_{Y,Q} for every Q in g^{−1}(Z); (3) every fiber of g has dimension less than m−1; and (4) the natural map O_X→Rg_*O_Y restricts to an isomorphism over U. Then O_X→Rg_*O_Y is an isomorphism globally.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The assertion is local on X, so take X affine. Conditions (1) and (2) imply depth_Z O_X ≥ m and depth_{g^{−1}Z}O_Y ≥ m, so local cohomology H^i_Z(O_X) and H^i_{g^{−1}Z}(O_Y) vanish for i≤m−1. The exact sequence of local cohomology then identifies H^i(Y,O_Y) with H^i(g^{−1}U,O_Y) and H^i(X,O_X) with H^i(U,O_X) for i<m−1. Condition (4) identifies the cohomology over g^{−1}U with that over U. Condition (3) gives H^i(Y,O_Y)=0 for i≥m−1. Since X is affine, H^i(X,O_X)=0 for i>0. Combining these gives H^i(Y,O_Y)=0 for all i>0 and H^0(Y,O_Y)=H^0(X,O_X), which is exactly Rg_*O_Y=O_X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Nested Hilbert scheme geometric inputs, Proposition 3.5.2, Proposition 3.5.3, Proposition 3.6.3, and Proposition 3.6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_{n−1,n} be the reduced closed subscheme of H_{n−1}×H_n consisting of pairs (I_{n−1},I_n) with I_n⊂I_{n−1}. The paper uses the theorem that H_{n−1,n} is nonsingular and irreducible of dimension 2n. Let α:H_{n−1,n}→F be the morphism to the universal family over H_n sending (I_{n−1},I_n) to (I_n,P), where P is the distinguished added point in σ(I_n). If d is the dimension of the fiber of α over (I,P), and r is the multiplicity of P in σ(I), then r ≥ binom(d+2,2). The canonical sheaf of H_n is trivial, ω_{H_n}≅O_{H_n}, while the canonical sheaf of H_{n−1,n} is O(1,−1), where O(k,l) denotes the tensor product of the pullbacks of O(k) from H_{n−1} and O(l) from H_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Smoothness and irreducibility of H_{n−1,n} are cited from Tikhomirov/Cheah. For the fiber estimate, the possible ideals I_{n−1} over fixed (I,P) correspond to one-dimensional subspaces in the socle of the Artin local ring (C[x,y]/I)_P. Thus d+1 is the socle dimension. Among length-r Artin local rings generated by two elements, the maximal possible socle dimension s satisfies r ≥ binom(s+1,2), seen by degenerating to monomial ideals and counting corners of Young diagrams. Setting s=d+1 gives the inequality. For canonical sheaves, Haiman covers the curvilinear locus by open sets U_x and U_y where C[x,y]/I is generated by x or y. On U_x, coordinates are the elementary symmetric functions e_i of x_1,...,x_n and interpolation coefficients a_i expressing y as a polynomial in x; the invariant form dx_1...dx_n dy_1...dy_n becomes da de, so ω_{H_n} is trivial, and the complement has codimension at least two. For H_{n−1,n}, the extra distinguished point produces local coordinates and a line bundle L=O(−1,1) with fiber I_{n−1}/I_n; comparing the local top forms on U_x and U_y shows ω_{H_{n−1,n}}≅L^{−1}=O(1,−1), again extending across codimension two.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 and Theorem 4, polygraph freeness and common ideal bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a field of characteristic zero, E=A^2(k), and consider E^n×E^l with coordinates x_j,y_j for the n factors of E^n and a_i,b_i for the l factors of E^l. For each function f:[l]→[n], let W_f be the graph defined by a_i=x_{f(i)} and b_i=y_{f(i)} for all i. The polygraph Z(n,l) is the reduced union ⋃_f W_f, and R(n,l)=O(Z(n,l)). Theorem 3 states that R(n,l) is a free module over k[y_1,...,y_n]. The stronger Theorem 4 defines, for integers m,r,k, subspace arrangements Y(m,r,k) inside Z(n,l), with ideals I(m,r,k), by taking the union of V(x_j:j in T)∩W_f over all f and all T⊂[n] satisfying |T∩[r]∖f([k])|≥m. It asserts that R(n,l) has a k[y]-basis B such that every ideal I(m,r,k) is spanned by a subset of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a long induction on n and l. The base l=0 is the polynomial ring k[x,y], with monomial basis and monomial ideals. The central local device is to work over open sets in Spec k[y]: U_1, where all y_i are distinct, and U_2, where at most one equality y_p=y_q occurs. Over U_1, the components W_f are disjoint. Over U_2∖U_1, the local geometry reduces to E^{[n]∖N}×Z(N,L) with |N|=2, so all nontrivial local checks reduce to n=2. A Hartogs-type lemma for torsion-free k[y]-modules says that a candidate basis which is a basis after localizing at every point of U_2 is a global basis, since the complement has codimension two. The case n=2 is worked out explicitly: basis elements p[e,f] indexed by e∈N^2 and f:[l]→[2] are constructed, and the ideals I(m,r,k) are shown to be spanned by precisely those p[e,f] satisfying |[r]∖({j:e_j>0}∪f([k]))|<m. The induction uses partial elimination ideals for finite algebras R[b]/J, allowing a common ideal basis in a base ring to be lifted to one extra coordinate. Special arrangements Z′(r,k) are introduced so that their partial elimination ideals are locally the ideals I(m,r,k−1). Finally, three recursive steps construct bases: from R(n−1,l) to R(n,l)/I(1,1,l); descending from R(n,l)/I(1,1,t) to R(n,l)/I(1,1,t−1); and from R(n,l)/I(1,1,0) to R(n,l). The explicit n=2 analysis verifies the local radical and elimination identities needed in the lifting steps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8.1 and Corollaries 3.8.2-3.8.3, freeness of powers of the alternant ideal and flatness over one set of variables
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S=C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n], let A=S^ε be the space of S_n-alternating polynomials, and let J=SA be the ideal generated by A. Proposition 3.8.1 states that J^d is a free C[y_1,...,y_n]-module for every d≥0. Consequently, the projection X_n→C^n=Spec C[y_1,...,y_n] is flat. Another consequence is the ideal identity J^d = ∩_{i<j}(x_i−x_j,y_i−y_j)^d for all n and d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For d fixed, set l=nd and consider the polygraph Z(n,l). Let G=S_n^d act on the l pairs of a,b variables in d consecutive blocks of length n. By Theorem 3, R(n,l) is free over C[y]. Its G-alternating part R(n,l)^ε is a direct summand and hence also free over C[y]. Restrict functions from Z(n,l) to the component W_{f_0} where f_0(kn+i)=i. This sends R(n,l)^ε onto C[x,y]A^d=J^d. A G-alternating function on Z(n,l) is determined by its restriction to this component, because it vanishes on components where some block has repeated f-values and other relevant components are G-translates of W_{f_0}. Thus R(n,l)^ε≅J^d, proving freeness. The flatness of X_n over Spec C[y] follows because X_n=Proj S[tJ] and the Rees algebra has homogeneous pieces J^d free over C[y]. The intersection formula for J^d is quoted as following from the freeness result together with Haiman's earlier Proposition 6.2; it identifies powers of the alternant ideal with the intersection of powers of all pairwise diagonal ideals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.9, Frobenius-series identification mechanism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For R_µ=C[x,y]/J_µ, define its bigraded Frobenius series by F_{R_µ}(x;q,t)=∑_{r,s} t^r q^s Ψ(ch((R_µ)_{r,s})), where Ψ sends an S_n-character to its Frobenius characteristic and Ψ(χ_λ)=s_λ. The paper proves F_{R_µ}(x;q,t)=H̃_µ(x;q,t), equivalently the Schur coefficients of H̃_µ are the bigraded irreducible multiplicities in R_µ, hence also in D_µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the local ring S_µ=O_{X_n,Q_µ}. Flatness and fiber identification give F_{S_µ}=H(q,t)F_{R_µ} for an explicit scalar H(q,t), the Hilbert series of O_{H_n,I_µ}; H(q,t) is the reciprocal of a product over cells x of D(µ) involving arm and leg lengths, ∏(1−q^{−a(x)}t^{1+l(x)})∏(1−q^{1+a(x)}t^{−l(x)}). Since y_1,...,y_n form a regular sequence in S_µ, quotienting by y corresponds to plethystic substitution X→X(1−q). The quotient R_µ/(y) is identified with the Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber ring, whose Frobenius series is the q=0 specialization of H̃_µ and whose irreducible constituents satisfy dominance λ≥µ. This gives the first triangular condition for H̃_µ; switching x and y gives the second triangular condition; and the invariant part condition gives normalization. Proposition 2.1.1 then forces equality with H̃_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6, identification with the S_n-Hilbert scheme and character sheaf interpretation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V=(C^2)^n and G=S_n acting by permuting the n factors. Let V∥G denote the G-Hilbert scheme, the component of Hilb^{n!}(V) parametrizing G-orbits. Then the natural morphism φ:(C^2)^n∥S_n → H_n is an isomorphism, and under this isomorphism the universal family over the G-Hilbert scheme is identified with the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n. If P=ρ_*O_{X_n} is the tautological rank n! bundle on H_n≅V∥G and V^λ is the irreducible S_n-module with character χ_λ, then the character sheaf C_λ=Hom_{S_n}(V^λ,P) has rank dim V^λ. The transformed Kostka-Macdonald coefficient H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) is the T^2-character, equivalently the doubly graded Hilbert series, of the fiber of C_λ at the torus-fixed point I_µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first morphism, take the universal family Y over (C^2)^n∥S_n. The quotient Y/S_{n−1} is a flat family of degree n naturally embedded as a family of subschemes of C^2; the universal property of H_n gives φ. Conversely, Theorem 1 implies that X_n is flat of degree n! over H_n, so X_n defines a morphism H_n→Hilb^{n!}((C^2)^n), whose image lies in the S_n-Hilbert scheme. The two morphisms are inverse on the generic locus of distinct points, hence inverse everywhere by irreducibility. The character sheaf statement follows by decomposing P as ⊕_λ C_λ⊗V^λ and evaluating at the torus-fixed point I_µ; Proposition 3.7.3 identifies this fiber with R_µ, and Theorem 2 computes its graded irreducible multiplicities as H̃K_{λµ}(q,t).

proof_techniques.name: Realizing graded multiplicities as Hilbert-scheme fibers
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts the algebraic module R_µ=C[x,y]/J_µ, dual to the derivative span D_µ, into the scheme-theoretic fiber of the finite morphism ρ:X_n→H_n over the monomial ideal I_µ. This is achieved by describing X_n inside the n-fold universal family F^n using the alternation pairing B^{⊗n}⊗B^{⊗n}→∧^nB and then specializing the pairing at I_µ. Flatness and Cohen-Macaulayness of X_n imply that all fibers have the expected length n!, and Gorensteinness identifies the fiber ideal exactly with the differential annihilator J_µ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Schur coefficients in multigraded coinvariant algebras, one can try to realize the relevant coefficients as graded fibers or isotypic components of tautological or character sheaves on a Hilbert or G-Hilbert scheme. Hook coefficients might then be isolated by exterior-power or sign-twisted pieces of such bundles.

proof_techniques.name: Frobenius characteristic plus Macdonald triangularity recognition
proof_techniques.description: Rather than compute every irreducible multiplicity directly, Haiman packages the bigraded S_n-character into a Frobenius series F(x;q,t). He proves that F satisfies the same two plethystic triangularity conditions and normalization as H̃_µ. The triangularity is obtained from a simpler quotient, R_µ/(y), already known through the Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber model, while the second condition follows by x/y symmetry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the universal coefficients c_λ(n) are Schur coefficients of a stable Hilbert series, a comparable tactic is to identify a Frobenius or symmetric-function series and prove it satisfies a characterization whose hook coefficients are known or easier to extract after a specialization.

proof_techniques.name: Alternation pairing and coefficient-of-determinant test
proof_techniques.description: The ideal J_µ is characterized by testing whether Θ_ε(gp) has zero Δ_µ coefficient for every g. Geometrically, the same test appears as the kernel of the alternation pairing on B^{⊗n}. This turns a differential-annihilator question into a statement about alternating components and determinant bases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are closely tied to exterior powers and alternating constructions. This coefficient-testing method suggests looking for determinant or alternant bases in m-set coinvariant algebras and characterizing hook multiplicities through projections onto alternating or near-alternating components.

proof_techniques.name: Polygraph freeness via codimension-two local checking
proof_techniques.description: The polygraph theorem proves freeness over one set of variables by constructing a common ideal basis. A crucial local-to-global principle says that for torsion-free k[y]-modules it suffices to verify a proposed basis after localization over the open set where at most two y-coordinates coincide, because the complement has codimension two. Over this open set, the local geometry reduces to the n=2 case, which is explicitly computable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For m-set diagonal or generalized coinvariant algebras, freeness or stability over one grading set may be approachable by reducing local collisions to two-particle calculations. This is especially relevant if c_λ(n) is independent of m because such independence often reflects a hidden free-module or stable-basis structure.

proof_techniques.name: Common ideal bases for arrangements
proof_techniques.description: Haiman strengthens plain freeness of R(n,l) to the existence of a basis simultaneously adapted to many subspace-arrangement ideals I(m,r,k). The induction requires carefully designed arrangements Y(m,r,k) whose ideals form a controlled lattice locally, and whose quotients remain torsion-free or free over k[y]. This allows successive extensions of bases while retaining compatibility with all needed ideals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook coefficient formula may require tracking not just the full Hilbert series but a filtration or family of quotients corresponding to descent, support, or exterior-power conditions. A common-basis framework could organize such filtrations and make combinatorial indexing possible.

proof_techniques.name: Partial elimination ideals for lifting bases
proof_techniques.description: Given a finite algebra S=R[b]/J over a base ring R, Haiman uses the partial elimination ideals I_m consisting of leading coefficients of relations of degree m−1 in b. Geometrically, V(I_m) records where the fiber of Spec S→Spec R has length at least m. If R has a common ideal basis compatible with these I_m, one lifts it to a basis of S by adjoining 1,b,...,b^{m−1} to the appropriate basis elements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an m-set coinvariant algebra is built by adding one set of variables at a time, partial elimination ideals could give an inductive way to lift bases and track how Schur or hook coefficients change when m increases.

proof_techniques.name: Nested Hilbert scheme induction and duality
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Gorensteinness of X_n uses X_{n−1,n} as an intermediary between X_{n−1} and X_n. After proving the nested scheme is Cohen-Macaulay and computing its canonical bundle, Haiman proves Rg_*O=O for the projection to X_n using a regular-sequence/local-cohomology lemma. Grothendieck duality then transports the dualizing sheaf from the nested scheme to X_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For modules whose Hilbert series satisfy recursions in n, nested Hilbert schemes or analogous induction correspondences may supply structural recurrences. Hook-shape coefficients often admit branching rules, so an n−1 to n geometric correspondence could be relevant.

proof_techniques.name: Use of q=0 Springer-fiber/Garsia-Procesi model as a base case
proof_techniques.description: The two-parameter Macdonald character formula is proved by reducing one triangularity condition to the q=0 quotient R_µ/(y), which was already understood as a Springer-fiber cohomology ring by Garsia-Procesi. This imports known Hall-Littlewood/t-Kostka positivity and dominance restrictions into the two-variable setting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients of multigraded coinvariant algebras, one may seek a one-variable specialization with a known combinatorial model, such as major index, descent sets, or Springer theory, and then lift the result to the multigraded or stable Schur expansion.

proof_techniques.name: Character sheaves on the G-Hilbert scheme
proof_techniques.description: After identifying H_n with (C^2)^n∥S_n, the tautological bundle P decomposes according to irreducible S_n-representations as P=⊕_λ C_λ⊗V^λ. The fiber of C_λ at the fixed point I_µ has T^2-character H̃K_{λµ}(q,t). This gives a geometric vector-bundle interpretation of Schur coefficients in transformed Macdonald polynomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The universal coefficients c_λ(n) in Schur expansions may similarly be interpretable as Hilbert series or dimensions of fibers of character sheaves, with hook λ corresponding to specific irreducible representations such as exterior powers of the reflection representation.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly discusses the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n=C[x,y]/I_n, where I_n is generated by S_n-invariant polynomials without constant term; it states that R_n has the same graded character as diagonal harmonics and recalls conjectures, not proved in this paper, that dim R_n=(n+1)^{n−1} and that bases should be indexed by parking functions. Section 5.3 emphasizes that Theorem 2 proves positivity and the n! theorem but does not give an explicit combinatorial basis for R_µ or D_µ; Problem 5.3.1 asks for such a basis giving a purely combinatorial interpretation of H̃K_{λµ}(q,t). Problem 5.3.2 asks for a doubly homogeneous S_n-compatible basis of the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n with distinguished subsets giving bases of each R_µ. Problem 5.3.3 relates the polygraph basis of R(n,n) to a hoped-for parking-function basis of R_n. Section 5.2 proposes a higher-dimensional polygraph freeness conjecture for E=A^d, namely that the coordinate ring of the corresponding polygraph is free over one set of variables; this is potentially relevant to coinvariant algebras with more than two sets of variables. The paper itself contains no hook-shape combinatorial interpretation for the universal coefficients c_λ(n) in the m-set coinvariant Hilbert series.
why_relevant: Proves the n! conjecture and Macdonald positivity using Hilbert schemes and isospectral Hilbert schemes. These geometric methods underpin the positivity and representation-theoretic interpretation of diagonal coinvariant modules.
